splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA must be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or may be selected afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current information in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises fundamental questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee absconds, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original term.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release continues to run even while the supervisee is in absconder status, and courts lack authority to extend the term absent explicit statutory authorization.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the additional gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1) — which categorically bars claims previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are barred from re-raising any previously presented claim; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, requiring only that they satisfy the requirements of § 2255(h).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. A broad standing rule would make it far easier to challenge election rules nationwide, while denying standing pre-election would funnel disputes into post-election litigation with severe timing pressures.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that allegedly dilute lawful votes or alter election outcomes, even before Election Day.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's alleged competitive injury from a state election law is too speculative or generalized to confer Article III standing for a pre-election challenge.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that carry criminal consequences.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether courts should apply lenity or defer to the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant interpretation when Guidelines provisions are ambiguous. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference prevails over lenity. The issue has gained significance after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency interpretations in criminal or sentencing contexts, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary) takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (requiring only one).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision. This determines whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief (allowing courts to sentence below statutory minimums) only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria, or if possessing any single criterion is disqualifying. The split leads to dramatically different sentencing outcomes depending on geography.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary (Application Note 1) may authoritatively expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2 — which adds conspiracy, aiding and abetting, and attempt to the definition of 'controlled substance offense' — is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text itself is silent on inchoate offenses. This affects whether prior conspiracy convictions trigger the career-offender enhancement.

positions.label: Commentary is authoritative — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy and other inchoate offenses in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is an authoritative interpretation entitled to deference, and the guideline text is ambiguous.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly adds to or expands the guideline rather than interpreting it, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law can contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. The Eighth Circuit categorically bars consideration of non-retroactive changes in law; the Seventh Circuit holds the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment cannot serve as such a reason. Other circuits have permitted consideration of such changes, at least as one factor among others. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) sought to allow this, but circuits disagree on the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered, at least as one factor among others, when determining whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor, creating a direct split on the interpretation of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a), means that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) fixes the estate's property as of the petition date, and post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor upon good-faith conversion, consistent with the statute's structure and legislative history.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state statute covers substances not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the substance at issue to be federally controlled. The Eighth Circuit holds there is no federal-law limitation, while other circuits require a match to federal schedules. This split affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation — state schedules suffice
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' includes state offenses regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled, because there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Federal-law limitation applies — must match federal schedules
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the state statute is coextensive with or narrower than federal drug schedules, applying the categorical approach.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to conditions of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) through habeas corpus, or whether they may proceed via civil rights actions such as Bivens.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether challenges to conditions of confinement — as distinct from the fact or duration of imprisonment — are cognizable in habeas corpus or must be brought through civil rights actions. The Supreme Court left this question open in Preiser v. Rodriguez. This affects federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions, including First Step Act earned-time-credits determinations.

positions.label: Habeas is available for conditions-of-confinement claims
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is available to challenge conditions of confinement, including BOP decisions about placement and earned time credits, not just the fact or duration of confinement.

positions.label: Habeas limited to fact/duration of confinement
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is limited to challenges to the fact or duration of confinement, and conditions-of-confinement claims must be brought through civil rights actions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil appeal deadline (60 days when the government is a party) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days) applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is a civil matter subject to the 60-day appeal deadline or a criminal matter subject to the 14-day deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because a Hyde Amendment motion is ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline applies.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Triad Energy Corp.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises from and is part of the criminal case, so the criminal appeal deadline applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose sentences upon revocation of supervised release for the purpose of punishing the defendant for the violation, or only to address the breach of trust and promote the goals of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether supervised release revocation sentences may serve a punitive purpose. The Sixth Circuit precedent in United States v. Lewis allows courts expressly to punish defendants for violations of supervised release, while other circuits limit the purpose of revocation sentences to addressing the breach of the court's trust. Dissenting judges in the Sixth Circuit note this places their court at the extreme of a circuit split.

positions.label: Punishment is a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may revoke supervised release and impose sentences expressly to punish defendants for their violations.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should address the breach of the court's trust and promote the goals of supervised release, not serve as punishment for the underlying violation.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as 'effectively unreviewable' on appeal from a final judgment.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order.' This creates tension with other circuits that have more restrictively applied the collateral order doctrine to sealing motions.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records is effectively unreviewable after final judgment and involves a value of sufficiently 'high order' to satisfy the collateral order doctrine.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine because the privacy interest can be adequately protected through other means or vindicable on appeal from final judgment.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Shane Group, Inc. v. Blue Cross Blue Shield

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any . . . broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries caused by motor carriers they selected. This affects liability for trucking accidents nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services and thus preempted by the FAAAA's broad preemption provision.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that generally applicable state tort law does not fall within the FAAAA's preemption clause because it does not specifically target broker services related to price, route, or service.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus relief — such as alleged legal errors in the underlying conviction — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under the compassionate release statute. Some circuits permit consideration while others categorically bar it.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts have discretion to consider a broad range of factors, including potential legal errors in the conviction or sentence, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for challenging the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or related civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in hospitality settings, indicating a circuit split on the applicable legal standards for such claims. The case will clarify standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages in service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied a particular legal framework to discrimination claims arising from hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Differing approach by other circuits
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards to discrimination claims in commercial or hospitality contexts, creating the split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances, particularly marijuana, is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and the nation's historical tradition of firearms regulation.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming those who pose a danger to themselves or others, including habitual substance users, supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) even after Bruen.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government cannot demonstrate a sufficient historical analogue for disarming non-violent drug users.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
